main_ingredients: wood ear fungus (black fungus, Auricularia auricula-judae)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of crisp wood ear fungus salad as a side dish or appetizer is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion as commonly served in Chinese restaurants and food delivery contexts in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mushrooms, wood ear, dried (SR Legacy ID 168436) and Mushrooms, wood ear, raw; combined with condiment composition estimates from HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Wood ear fungus (Auricularia auricula-judae) nutrient base derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for dried wood ear mushroom (rehydrated equivalent) and raw wood ear mushroom. Dressing components (soy sauce, sesame oil, black vinegar, garlic, chilli, sugar) estimated using HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database entries for individual condiments. Final per-100g composite values are consistent with the input record (energy 53 kcal, protein 1.67 g, fat 2.67 g, carbohydrate 6.0 g, sodium 300 mg) and are retained as the best available estimate for this prepared dish. Cholesterol confirmed 0 mg (plant-based dish). Trans fat confirmed 0 g. Vitamin C confirmed 0 mg for this preparation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Mushrooms (analogous estimate); University of Sydney GI Database — mushroom category reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crisp wood ear fungus salad. GI estimated at approximately 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature for mushrooms and low-carbohydrate fungal foods, which consistently show GI values in the 10–20 range. Wood ear fungus is predominantly water, dietary fibre (beta-glucans), and low digestible carbohydrate, supporting a very low GI estimate. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (15 × 6.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.35 g, classified as very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 300 mg per 100 g from soy sauce in the dressing
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or low-sodium soy sauce to bring sodium below 200 mg per 100 g; the black vinegar and garlic components provide cardiovascular benefit and should be maintained.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15), low sugar (2.0 g per 100 g), and high dietary fibre (3.33 g per 100 g) from wood ear fungus beta-glucans
type2_diabetes.guidance: Crisp wood ear fungus salad is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a low-GI, high-fibre side dish; the black vinegar component has evidence-based post-meal glucose-lowering properties, and pairing with a lean protein source will further stabilise postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.33 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, and beta-glucan fibre from wood ear fungus supporting lipid modulation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is favourable for cardiovascular health due to negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and the presence of beta-glucan fibre which supports LDL reduction; sodium from soy sauce should be monitored and reduced where possible for individuals with concurrent hypertension.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beta-glucans in wood ear fungus modulate immune response; garlic and sesame oil provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols and PUFA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Wood ear fungus salad is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis management; individuals sensitive to nightshades should monitor their response to chilli in the dressing, and reduced soy sauce is recommended to keep sodium within RA dietary thresholds.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crisp Wood Ear Fungus Salad (凉拌木耳, Liáng bàn mù ěr) is a popular Chinese cold appetizer or side dish made from Auricularia auricula-judae, commonly known as wood ear fungus or black fungus. The fungus is briefly blanched in boiling water to achieve a characteristic crisp, crunchy, and slightly gelatinous texture, then immediately chilled and tossed in a tangy, savoury dressing typically composed of soy sauce, Chinese black vinegar (Chinkiang vinegar), sesame oil, minced garlic, chilli, and a small amount of sugar. The result is a refreshing, low-calorie dish with a complex umami-sour-spicy flavour profile. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 53 kcal per 100 g, with 1.67 g protein, 2.67 g fat (predominantly from sesame oil, with only 0.33 g saturated fat), 6.0 g carbohydrate, 2.0 g sugar, and 3.33 g dietary fibre per 100 g. The dietary fibre is primarily beta-glucan from the wood ear fungus, which has evidence-based benefits for immune modulation, LDL cholesterol reduction, and postprandial glucose attenuation. The dish is entirely plant-based (vegan and vegetarian), contains no cholesterol, and is free of dairy. Sodium is 300 mg per 100 g, primarily from soy sauce, which is the main dietary concern for individuals with hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (very low), making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Black vinegar has peer-reviewed evidence supporting post-meal blood glucose reduction. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a cold starter in Chinese restaurants, zi char stalls, and is available through food delivery platforms. A standard serving is approximately 150 g. The dish is culturally associated with Chinese cuisine and is widely consumed across Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for wood ear mushroom combined with HPB Singapore condiment composition estimates. The input record values (energy 53 kcal, protein 1.67 g, fat 2.67 g, carbohydrate 6.0 g, sodium 300 mg) are consistent with this source combination and are retained. No exact match for this specific prepared dish exists in a single approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database mushroom category. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this prepared wood ear fungus dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as of 2024; sesame oil contributes PUFA but exact per-100g dish-level values cannot be reliably disaggregated. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific prepared dish is not reported in the adapted sources; wood ear fungus contains potassium but dish-level composite value is unavailable. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite prepared dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database at the dish level as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — this is a plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats; sesame oil and soy sauce do not contribute trans fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — entirely plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed trace/negligible — wood ear fungus and the dressing components do not contribute meaningful vitamin C. Input ethnic_tags ('Drinks & Beverages', 'Noodles') appear to be data entry errors and have been excluded from the output as they are inconsistent with this dish. Input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not directly support the nutrient values claimed; nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore as approved sources.
